title: MsgDecodeError omits partialFormat — consumer substitutes DecodeOptions.format which may not match the partial pixels
description: MsgDecodeError carries partialPixels but no partialFormat field. The session consumer (decode-session.ts:344) injects this.opts.format instead of the format the worker actually used to produce the partial pixels. If the worker or WASM bridge downcasted the format (e.g., requested rgbaf32 but libjxl produced rgba8), the consumer builds a DecodeFrameEvent with the wrong format, causing callers to misinterpret pixel data. MsgDecodeProgress and MsgDecodeFinal both carry an explicit format field — this is an asymmetry with no documented justification.

title: EncodeOptions fields modular/brotliEffort/decodingSpeed/photonNoise/jpegReconstruction/alreadyDownsampled/upsamplingMode/ecResampling/frameIndexing/allowExpertOptions/buffering are declared but never forwarded in MsgEncodeStart
description: EncodeOptions (types.ts) declares many fields — modular, brotliEffort, decodingSpeed, photonNoiseIso, jpegReconstruction, alreadyDownsampled, upsamplingMode, ecResampling, frameIndexing, allowExpertOptions, and buffering — that have no corresponding fields in MsgEncodeStart (protocol.ts lines 173-229). EncodeSessionImpl (encode-session.ts lines 65-96) only copies a small subset into startMsg; the rest are silently dropped. Callers setting these options get no error and no effect, a silent contract violation. The encode-handler at the worker side has no way to apply them.

title: PixelFormat union includes 'rgb8' which is documented as encode-only, but DecodeOptions.format is typed as PixelFormat — no guard prevents passing 'rgb8' to a decode
description: PixelFormat is a single union ('rgba8'|'rgba16'|'rgbaf32'|'rgb8') and the comment on 'rgb8' states 'encode input only'. However, DecodeOptions.format (types.ts line 57) accepts the full PixelFormat union. No runtime guard in DecodeSessionImpl, the decode handler, or the facade rejects format='rgb8' on decode. This means callers can silently request an impossible output format; the WASM bridge has no decode path for rgb8, leading to an unspecified runtime failure. A dedicated DecodePixelFormat type should exclude 'rgb8'.
code_excerpt:
export type PixelFormat =
  | "rgba8"     // 4 channels, 8-bit, premultiplied alpha = false
  | "rgba16"    // 4 channels, 16-bit
  | "rgbaf32"   // 4 channels, 32-bit float (linear)
  | "rgb8";     // encode input only: 3 channels, 8-bit, no alpha (skips RGBA round-trip)

// DecodeOptions.format accepts the full union:
  format: PixelFormat;              // requested output format

title: EncodeSession.done() declares return type Promise<number> but the contract comment says 'resolves with total bytes written' — this is redundant with getStats().compressedBytes and the two can diverge
description: EncodeSession.done() returns total bytes as a number, while getStats().compressedBytes also carries this value. In EncodeSessionImpl, complete() sets totalBytesWritten from msg.totalBytes and resolves doneDeferred with the same value. If totalBytesWritten is set before getStats() is called, the two should agree; however, if a caller reads getStats() before done() resolves (partial completion), it returns null — creating an inconsistency window. The interface should document the ordering invariant explicitly, or done() should resolve with EncodeStats directly.

title: EncodeStats.ratio can produce Infinity or NaN when originalBytes is 0 (zero-dimension encode)
description: EncodeStats.ratio is defined as compressedBytes / originalBytes. EncodeSessionImpl computes rawProduct = width * height * bpp. When width or height is 0, rawProduct is 0 and ratio becomes Infinity (or NaN if compressedBytes is also 0). No guard exists in the EncodeSessionImpl.getStats() path. A ratio of Infinity silently passes the TypeScript type check but breaks any consumer that serializes stats to JSON or applies arithmetic to ratio.

title: WorkerToMainMessage and MainToWorkerMessage unions have no round-trip serialization tests — protocol schema can drift between browser and node workers silently
description: The protocol types are TypeScript interfaces with no runtime validation. Browser and node workers each construct messages from the same protocol.ts, but there are no tests that assert a message emitted by one worker variant is accepted by the other. Fields like MsgWorkerDrain.adaptiveHwm and MsgDecodeFinal.sidecarOffsets are optional and only tested in isolation. A schema validation library or a cross-worker message fuzz test would catch drift early.
code_excerpt:
export type WorkerToMainMessage =
  | MsgDecodeHeader
  | MsgDecodeProgress
  | MsgDecodeFinal
  | MsgDecodeError
  | MsgDecodeCancelled
  | MsgDecodePaused
  | MsgDecodeBudgetExceeded
  | MsgEncodeChunk
  | MsgEncodeFirstByteReady
  | MsgEncodeDone
  | MsgEncodeError
  | MsgEncodeCancelled
  | MsgWorkerReady
  | MsgWorkerShutdownAck
  | MsgWorkerError
  | MsgWorkerDrain
  | MsgMetric;

title: DecodeSession.push() and close() have no documented idempotency or ordering invariant for callers who call close() before all push() promises resolve
description: The DecodeSession interface documents push() as 'resolves when worker has accepted the chunk' and close() as 'signal end of input', but there is no documented contract about what happens if close() is called while a push() is still pending, or if push() is called after close(). The implementation handles this via this.terminated/this.closed guards, but the interface contract is silent. Consumers that race close() with the final push() may encounter surprising rejection behavior.

title: JxlErrorCode 'QueueOverflow' is present in decode-session's KNOWN_JXL_ERROR_CODES but the encode-session's equivalent set independently redeclares it — dual maintenance risk
description: Both decode-session.ts and encode-session.ts each maintain their own KNOWN_JXL_ERROR_CODES Set<JxlErrorCode> hardcoding all 11 error codes. These must be kept in sync with the JxlErrorCode union in errors.ts. When a new code is added to JxlErrorCode, both sets must be updated manually; TypeScript does not enforce exhaustiveness here. A single export from jxl-core (e.g., a const array or derived Set) would eliminate this.
